name: Tromsø International Film Festival (TIFF)
description: One of Scandinavia's leading film festivals, screening 150+ films across venues in Tromsø during the polar night. TIFF focuses on international cinema, Norwegian premieres, and Arctic-themed documentaries. Screenings run from morning to midnight in the darkest week of the year.
imageAlt: Tromsø International Film Festival screening during polar night

name: Nordlysfestivalen (Northern Lights Festival)
description: A week-long music festival in Tromsø combining classical, jazz, opera, and contemporary performances. Founded in 1988, it runs during the polar night when the aurora is most active. Concerts take place in Ishavskatedralen (Arctic Cathedral) and venues across the city.
imageAlt: Northern Lights Festival concert at the Arctic Cathedral in Tromsø
frequency: Annual, late January to early February

name: Polar Jazz
description: The world's northernmost jazz festival, held in Longyearbyen at 78°N. Four days of concerts in intimate venues across Svalbard's only town, with performers from across the Nordic countries. The backdrop is complete polar darkness and potential aurora sightings between sets.
imageAlt: Polar Jazz festival performance in Longyearbyen, Svalbard

name: Sami Easter Festival (Påskefestivalen)
description: The largest annual gathering of Sami culture, held in Kautokeino during Easter week. Includes the Sami Grand Prix (joik competition), reindeer racing on the frozen Kautokeino river, Sami film festival, art exhibitions, and traditional duodji craft markets. This is a Sami-led, Sami-run event. Attend as a respectful guest.
imageAlt: Reindeer racing during Sami Easter Festival in Kautokeino
venue: Kautokeino centre, frozen river, cultural venues

name: Vossajazz
description: Norway's oldest jazz festival (founded 1973), held in the mountain town of Voss. Four days of jazz, improv, and experimental music across intimate venues. Voss has 14,000 residents and the festival fills every hotel and guesthouse. Book accommodation by February or sleep in your car.
imageAlt: Vossajazz concert performance in Voss, western Norway

name: Bergen International Festival (Festspillene)
description: Norway's largest arts festival, running since 1953. Fifteen days of music, theatre, dance, opera, and visual art across Bergen's historic venues. Performances in Grieghallen, Håkonshallen, and outdoor stages across Bryggen. The festival coincides with the arrival of long Nordic spring evenings.
imageAlt: Bergen International Festival performance at Grieghallen
frequency: Annual, late May to early June

name: Midnight Sun Marathon
description: Run a marathon under the midnight sun in Tromsø. The course follows the waterfront and crosses the Tromsø Bridge with views of the Arctic Cathedral. Start time is 20:30 so runners finish in full midnight daylight. Half marathon and 10K options available. 7,000 runners from 70 countries.
imageAlt: Runners crossing Tromsø Bridge during the Midnight Sun Marathon

name: Ekstremsportveko (Extreme Sports Week)
description: A week of skydiving, BASE jumping, paragliding, kayaking, and mountain biking in and around Voss. 12,000 participants descend on a town of 14,000. Free to watch many events. If you have not booked accommodation by February, you will not find any. The festival runs regardless of weather.
imageAlt: BASE jumper during Ekstremsportveko in Voss, Norway
venue: Voss and surrounding mountains, rivers, and airspace

name: Gladmatfestivalen (Gladmat Food Festival)
description: Scandinavia's largest food festival, held on the Stavanger harbour. Four days of chef demonstrations, food stalls, and tastings from 200+ producers. Local seafood, Rogaland lamb, and craft beverages dominate. Free entry to the harbour area. Individual tastings and events are ticketed.
imageAlt: Gladmat food festival stalls along Stavanger harbour

name: Riddu Riđđu Festival
description: An indigenous music and arts festival in Kåfjord, Troms, founded and run by the Sami community since 1991. Four days of concerts from indigenous artists worldwide, Sami joik performances, film screenings, and workshops. The festival grounds sit on the shores of the Kåfjord with midnight sun overhead. This is Sami-led and Sami-programmed. Attend on those terms.
imageAlt: Riddu Riđđu indigenous festival stage in Kåfjord under midnight sun

name: Olavsfestdagene (St. Olav Festival)
description: Trondheim's medieval heritage festival, centred on the Nidaros Cathedral and the legacy of St. Olav. Nine days of concerts, theatre, pilgrim walks, medieval markets, and church services. The festival dates back to the medieval Olsok celebrations (29 July). Performances range from Gregorian chant in the cathedral to outdoor rock on the Torvet square.
imageAlt: Olavsfestdagene medieval celebration at Nidaros Cathedral, Trondheim
venue: Nidaros Cathedral, Erkebispegården, Torvet, multiple venues

name: Lofoten International Art Festival (LIAF)
description: A biennial contemporary art festival spread across Lofoten's fishing villages. Exhibitions in rorbuer, fish factories, and public spaces from Svolvær to Å. The 2026 edition is curated around Arctic environmental themes. The art sits in working landscapes, not white-walled galleries. Free entry to most installations.
imageAlt: Art installation in a converted rorbu during Lofoten International Art Festival

name: Dark Season Blues
description: A blues festival in Longyearbyen held during the transition into polar night. Four days of live blues, rock, and roots music in the world's northernmost town at 78°N. The sun has already set for the last time, and the darkness is settling in. Intimate concerts in Kulturhuset and Huset. 1,500 capacity, sells out early.
imageAlt: Dark Season Blues concert in Longyearbyen during polar night

name: Trænafestivalen
description: 3,000 people on a remote island off the Helgeland coast, with Norway's second-oldest lighthouse as a backdrop. Three days of music on Træna, accessible only by boat or helicopter. The festival sells out in March. Camping on the island is the only accommodation option for most. The boat from Sandnessjøen takes 3.5 hours.
imageAlt: Trænafestivalen stage with the Helgeland coast and lighthouse behind
frequency: Annual, second weekend of July. Sells out in March.

name: Svalbard Ski Marathon (Svalbard Skimaraton)
description: A cross-country ski race across the Arctic tundra outside Longyearbyen. Distances of 20 km and 42 km through the Adventdalen valley at 78°N with snow-covered mountains on all sides. Armed polar bear guards patrol the course. Open to all fitness levels on the 20 km distance. Temperature at the start line averages -15°C.
imageAlt: Cross-country skiers racing across Arctic tundra near Longyearbyen, Svalbard

name: Solfestuka (The Sun Festival Week)
description: Longyearbyen celebrates the return of the sun after four months of polar night. The first direct sunlight hits the old hospital steps on March 8. A week of concerts, art exhibitions, outdoor events, and communal gatherings follows. The festival is free and community-driven. The symbolic moment when residents gather on the steps to feel the first rays is the emotional peak of the Svalbard calendar.
imageAlt: Residents of Longyearbyen gathering on the old hospital steps to welcome the return of the sun
venue: Longyearbyen town centre, old hospital steps
frequency: Annual, week of March 8 (sun return date)

name: Spitsbergen Marathon
description: The world's northernmost marathon at 78°N. Distances of 10 km, half marathon, and full marathon through the Adventdalen and Longyeardalen valleys. Armed polar bear guards along the entire course. The midnight sun lights the route around the clock in June. Average race-day temperature: 5°C. Limited to 300 runners.
imageAlt: Runners in the Spitsbergen Marathon with Arctic mountains and midnight sun in the background

name: Taste Svalbard
description: A food festival in Longyearbyen showcasing Arctic cuisine. Local chefs prepare dishes using seal, reindeer, ptarmigan, Arctic char, and foraged herbs from the tundra. Held at restaurants and pop-up venues across town. Svalbard Bryggeri provides Arctic-brewed beer. Three days of tastings, chef talks, and communal dinners. 78°N gastronomy at its most concentrated.
imageAlt: Arctic cuisine dishes at Taste Svalbard food festival in Longyearbyen

name: KunstPause Svalbard
description: An arts and culture festival during the transition from polar night to spring light. Exhibitions, performances, film screenings, and workshops across Longyearbyen. Artists from mainland Norway and international guests respond to the Arctic landscape, darkness, and isolation. Held in Galleri Svalbard, Kulturhuset, and temporary spaces. Small-scale and community-focused.
imageAlt: Art exhibition at KunstPause festival in Longyearbyen, Svalbard

name: Lofotfiske (Lofoten Cod Fishing Season)
description: The annual Arctic cod migration to Lofoten has shaped Northern Norwegian identity for 1,000 years. Skrei (migrating cod) arrive from the Barents Sea between January and April. Henningsvær, Stamsund, and Reine harbours fill with fishing boats. The cod drying racks (hjell) go up across the islands. This is not a ticketed event. It is the working rhythm of the archipelago. Visit the harbours at 06:00 to watch the boats return.
imageAlt: Cod drying racks in Lofoten with snow-covered mountains behind, skrei fishing season

name: Lofotr Viking Festival
description: A week-long Viking festival at the Lofotr Viking Museum in Borg, built on the site of the largest Viking Age longhouse ever found (83 metres). Re-enactors from across Europe demonstrate combat, blacksmithing, textile work, and Viking ship sailing on the adjacent lake. The longhouse feast on the final evening serves mead, roasted lamb, and stockfish. 10,000 visitors across the week.
imageAlt: Viking re-enactors at the Lofotr Viking Festival with the reconstructed longhouse at Borg, Lofoten

name: Lofoten Insomnia Festival
description: A music and arts festival in Henningsvær during the midnight sun season. Three days of concerts across the fishing village, with stages in converted fish processing plants and on the harbour docks. The sun does not set. Norwegian and international indie, electronic, and folk acts. Capacity limited to 1,500 to protect the village. Sells out by April.
imageAlt: Concert stage at Lofoten Insomnia Festival in Henningsvær harbour under the midnight sun
frequency: Annual, late June. Sells out by April.

name: Lofoten Stockfish Festival (Tørrfiskfestivalen)
description: A celebration of Lofoten’s stockfish (tørrfisk) tradition in Stamsund. Cooking demonstrations, stockfish tastings, fishing boat trips, and competitions for the best stockfish dish. The festival is run by local producers who have been drying cod on wooden racks for generations. Free entry to the market area. Cooking workshops require advance booking.
imageAlt: Stockfish drying racks and food stalls at the Tørrfiskfestivalen in Stamsund, Lofoten

name: Lofoten Skimo (Ski Mountaineering Race)
description: A ski mountaineering race across the steep peaks of Lofoten. Competitors skin up and ski down multiple summits in a single day with the Arctic Ocean visible on both sides. Distances range from 15 km to 40 km with 1,500 to 4,000 metres of vertical gain. The terrain is alpine, exposed, and serious. Open to experienced ski tourers only. 200 participants.
imageAlt: Ski mountaineers ascending a Lofoten peak with ocean and islands visible below

name: Norseman Xtreme Triathlon
description: The world’s hardest triathlon: 3.8 km swim across Hardangerfjord at 05:00 in darkness (jumping from a ferry), 180 km bike over Hardangervidda to 1,200 m, 42 km run finishing on the summit of Gaustatoppen (1,883 m). 250 competitors by lottery only. Applications open November. The black T-shirt (top 160 to the summit) versus the white T-shirt (alternative finish) is the race’s defining distinction. Spectating the Eidfjord swim start is free.
imageAlt: Swimmers jumping from a ferry into Hardangerfjord at dawn during Norseman Xtreme Triathlon
frequency: Annual, first Saturday of August. Lottery opens November.

name: Hardanger Apple Harvest (Hardanger Fruktfestival)
description: The Hardangerfjord valley produces 40% of Norway’s fruit. The harvest season runs from late August (plums and pears) through October (apples). Cideries along the Fv551 south shore open their farmgates: Aga Cider, Hardanger Saft og Siderfabrikk, and Noregs Cider. The Hardangertun market at Lofthus (third weekend of September) gathers 50 producers, 8,000 visitors, and outdoor concerts. Outside festival days, farm visits are unticketed and free.
imageAlt: Apple orchards along Hardangerfjord with fruit stalls and cidery farmgate signs
venue: Lofthus, Hardangerfjord and cideries along Fv551
frequency: Annual, late August to mid-October. Hardangertun market: third weekend of September.

name: Parkenfestivalen
description: Bodø’s main music festival, held in Rønvik park. Three days of Norwegian and international rock, pop, and electronic acts. 15,000 daily capacity. The festival runs under the midnight sun in August with the Lofoten Wall visible across the Vestfjord. Bodø is the gateway city for ferries to Lofoten. Combine the festival with a Moskenès ferry crossing.
imageAlt: Concert at Parkenfestivalen in Bodø with midnight sun and mountains in the background
